title: Multi-Timeframe Technical Analysis Framework
content:
When performing technical analysis, always apply a top-down multi-timeframe approach:

1. WEEKLY CHART (Macro Trend)
   - Identify the primary trend direction (uptrend/downtrend/ranging)
   - Mark major support and resistance zones
   - Note key moving averages: 50-week and 200-week MA
   - Identify long-term chart patterns (cup & handle, head & shoulders, etc.)

2. DAILY CHART (Intermediate Trend)
   - Confirm alignment with weekly trend
   - Identify swing highs and swing lows
   - Apply 20, 50, and 200-day exponential moving averages
   - Mark supply and demand zones from recent price history
   - Check volume trends: rising volume confirms trend, falling volume warns of reversal

3. 4H / 1H CHART (Entry Precision)
   - Find high-probability entry zones within the larger trend
   - Look for confluence of: EMA support/resistance + horizontal S/R + trendlines
   - Identify candlestick reversal patterns at key levels (hammer, engulfing, pin bar)

4. STRUCTURE RULES
   - In uptrends: buy at higher lows, avoid buying at resistance
   - In downtrends: sell at lower highs, avoid shorting at support
   - Sideways: buy at the bottom of range, sell at the top

5. OUTPUT FORMAT
   - Trend direction (weekly / daily / intraday)
   - Key support levels (3 levels)
   - Key resistance levels (3 levels)
   - Recommended entry zone
   - Stop loss placement logic
   - Target levels (minimum 1:2 risk/reward)

title: RSI + MACD Momentum Analysis Framework
content:
Framework for analysing momentum using RSI and MACD in combination:

1. RSI ANALYSIS (14-period)
   - Overbought: RSI > 70. Do NOT blindly short — in strong trends RSI stays overbought
   - Oversold: RSI < 30. Do NOT blindly buy — in downtrends RSI stays oversold
   - Neutral zone (40-60): trend is ambiguous, reduce position size
   - RSI Divergence (HIGH SIGNAL):
     * Bullish divergence: price makes lower low, RSI makes higher low → buy signal
     * Bearish divergence: price makes higher high, RSI makes lower high → sell signal
   - RSI Trendline Breaks: draw trendlines on RSI itself; a break often precedes price breaks

2. MACD ANALYSIS (12, 26, 9 settings)
   - Signal line crossover: MACD crosses above signal = bullish; below = bearish
   - Zero line cross: MACD crossing above zero confirms bullish momentum shift
   - Histogram expansion: increasing bars = strengthening trend
   - Histogram contraction: shrinking bars = momentum fading, possible reversal
   - MACD Divergence: same logic as RSI divergence, adds confirmation weight

3. CONFLUENCE SCORING
   Score +1 for each bullish signal, -1 for each bearish:
   - RSI direction vs 50
   - RSI trend (rising/falling)
   - MACD vs signal line
   - MACD vs zero line
   - Histogram direction
   Score ≥ +3: strong bullish momentum
   Score ≤ -3: strong bearish momentum
   Score -2 to +2: neutral, wait for clarity

4. OUTPUT FORMAT
   - RSI value and interpretation
   - MACD signal and histogram state
   - Divergence present? (yes/no, type)
   - Confluence score
   - Momentum bias (strongly bullish / bullish / neutral / bearish / strongly bearish)

title: Volume Profile and Market Structure Analysis
content:
Framework for volume-based market structure analysis:

1. VWAP ANALYSIS
   - Price above VWAP: institutions are net buyers, bullish bias intraday
   - Price below VWAP: institutions net sellers, bearish bias intraday
   - VWAP as dynamic support/resistance for mean-reversion setups
   - Anchored VWAP: anchor to significant events (earnings, breakouts) for swing trading

2. VOLUME PROFILE KEY LEVELS
   - Point of Control (POC): highest-volume price level — strong support/resistance
   - Value Area High (VAH): 70% of volume traded below this — key resistance
   - Value Area Low (VAL): 70% of volume traded above this — key support
   - Low Volume Nodes (LVN): price tends to pass through quickly — breakout zones
   - High Volume Nodes (HVN): price consolidates here — magnets for price

3. VOLUME INTERPRETATION
   - Breakout on HIGH volume: valid breakout, likely to continue
   - Breakout on LOW volume: suspect, often fails — wait for retest
   - Selling climax: massive volume spike on down day = exhaustion, potential reversal
   - Buying climax: massive volume spike on up day after extended rally = distribution
   - Rising price + rising volume = healthy trend
   - Rising price + falling volume = trend weakening, watch for reversal

4. INSTITUTIONAL ORDER FLOW SIGNALS
   - Dark pool prints: unusually large block trades off-exchange → smart money positioning
   - Options flow: unusual call/put volume ratio changes signal sentiment shifts
   - Short interest: high short interest + rising price = short squeeze potential

5. OUTPUT FORMAT
   - VWAP position and bias
   - Key volume profile levels (POC, VAH, VAL)
   - Volume trend assessment
   - Institutional activity signals observed
   - Overall market structure bias

title: Earnings Analysis Framework
content:
Structured framework for analysing company earnings reports:

1. HEADLINE NUMBERS
   - EPS (actual vs. estimate): beat/miss magnitude matters (>10% beat is significant)
   - Revenue (actual vs. estimate): revenue quality > EPS (EPS can be managed via buybacks)
   - Year-over-year growth rates for both metrics
   - Net income margin trend (expanding/contracting/stable)

2. QUALITY OF EARNINGS CHECK
   - Operating cash flow vs. net income: OCF should track or exceed net income
   - Accounts receivable growth vs. revenue growth: if AR grows faster → revenue quality concern
   - Inventory build vs. sales: rising inventory without sales growth = demand problem
   - One-time items: strip out non-recurring gains/charges to find normalised earnings

3. FORWARD GUIDANCE ANALYSIS (most important for stock price)
   - EPS guidance vs. analyst consensus: raised/maintained/lowered
   - Revenue guidance and growth trajectory
   - Management commentary tone: confident vs. cautious language signals
   - Key business metrics specific to the sector (ARR for SaaS, same-store sales for retail, etc.)

4. VALUATION CONTEXT
   - P/E ratio: compare to 3-year historical average and sector peers
   - EV/EBITDA: more useful for capital-intensive businesses
   - PEG ratio: P/E divided by growth rate — PEG < 1.0 is generally undervalued
   - Price/FCF: for cash-generative businesses, most reliable long-term indicator

5. POST-EARNINGS PRICE ACTION RULE
   - Gap up on good earnings: if stock holds gap, bullish continuation likely
   - Gap up then fill: institutions distributed into strength — bearish signal
   - Gap down on miss: if support holds, may be overreaction — watch for recovery
   - Sell the news: stock was priced for perfection, anything less = sell-off

6. OUTPUT FORMAT
   - EPS beat/miss summary
   - Revenue beat/miss summary
   - Earnings quality assessment (1-5 scale)
   - Guidance direction (raised/maintained/lowered)
   - Valuation verdict (cheap/fair/expensive relative to growth)
   - Recommended action thesis

title: Market Sentiment and Fear/Greed Analysis
content:
Framework for quantifying and interpreting market sentiment:

1. VIX (CBOE VOLATILITY INDEX)
   - VIX < 15: extreme complacency, markets often near short-term tops
   - VIX 15-20: normal, trend-following environment
   - VIX 20-30: elevated fear, hedging activity increases
   - VIX > 30: fear spike, often marks medium-term bottoms (buy opportunity)
   - VIX > 40: capitulation zone, historically excellent long-term entry
   - VIX Term Structure: VIX futures in backwardation (near > far) = acute fear spike

2. PUT/CALL RATIO
   - CBOE equity P/C ratio > 0.80: excessive bearishness, contrarian bullish
   - CBOE equity P/C ratio < 0.50: excessive bullishness, contrarian bearish
   - Spike in P/C > 1.0: panic hedging, often marks local bottoms
   - 10-day moving average of P/C more reliable than single-day readings

3. MARKET BREADTH INDICATORS
   - Advance/Decline Line: rising A/D line confirms bull market; divergence warns of weakness
   - % Stocks Above 200-day MA: > 70% = strong market; < 30% = oversold, potential bottom
   - New 52-week Highs vs. Lows: bull market = more highs than lows consistently
   - McClellan Oscillator: momentum measure of A/D; extremes signal reversal

4. FUND FLOWS AND POSITIONING
   - AAII Bull/Bear Survey: > 50% bulls = complacency; < 25% bulls = excessive pessimism
   - COT Report (Futures positioning): track Large Speculators vs. Commercials
   - ETF flows: money flowing into/out of sector ETFs signals institutional conviction
   - Retail investor margin debt: rising margin = speculation; sudden decline = forced selling

5. SENTIMENT COMPOSITE SCORE
   Build a composite:
   - VIX level (fear score)
   - P/C ratio (hedging score)
   - A/D Line direction
   - % stocks above 200-day MA
   Composite reading → overall sentiment: Extreme Fear / Fear / Neutral / Greed / Extreme Greed

6. OUTPUT FORMAT
   - VIX level and interpretation
   - P/C ratio reading
   - Breadth assessment
   - Composite sentiment score
   - Contrarian signal (if sentiment is at an extreme)

title: Macro Environment and Sector Rotation Framework
content:
Framework for analysing the macroeconomic environment and positioning sectors accordingly:

1. ECONOMIC CYCLE IDENTIFICATION
   Phases and sector leadership:
   - Early Expansion: Financials, Consumer Discretionary, Technology lead
   - Mid Expansion: Industrials, Materials, Energy lead
   - Late Expansion: Energy, Materials, Healthcare defensively balance
   - Recession: Utilities, Consumer Staples, Healthcare, Bonds outperform
   - Recovery: Technology, Financials, Consumer Discretionary re-emerge

2. INTEREST RATE ENVIRONMENT
   - Rising rates: Financials benefit; Real Estate, Utilities, and Bonds hurt
   - Falling rates: REITs, Utilities, long-duration growth stocks benefit
   - Yield curve (10yr - 2yr spread):
     * Steepening: economic expansion, financials outperform
     * Flattening: slowdown warning, rotate to defensives
     * Inverted: recession signal within 12-18 months historically
   - Real yields (TIPS): negative real yields favour gold and growth; positive favours value

3. DOLLAR STRENGTH IMPACT
   - Strong USD: headwind for large-cap multinationals, emerging markets struggle
   - Weak USD: tailwind for commodities, international stocks, gold
   - DXY above 105: defensive dollar positioning advised for global equities

4. INFLATION REGIME
   - High inflation: commodities, energy, TIPS, real assets outperform
   - Disinflation: growth stocks, long bonds, technology outperform
   - Deflation: cash, long-duration treasuries, consumer staples

5. LEADING ECONOMIC INDICATORS TO TRACK
   - ISM Manufacturing PMI: > 50 = expansion, < 50 = contraction
   - Conference Board LEI: direction signals economic turns 6-9 months early
   - Initial jobless claims: spike signals labour market stress
   - Credit spreads (HY vs. IG): widening spreads = risk-off signal

6. OUTPUT FORMAT
   - Current economic cycle phase
   - Interest rate regime and trend
   - Dollar environment assessment
   - Inflation regime
   - Favoured sectors given macro backdrop
   - Sectors to underweight / avoid

title: Position Sizing and Risk Management Framework
content:
Framework for disciplined position sizing and portfolio risk management:

1. POSITION SIZING RULES
   - Maximum risk per trade: 1-2% of total portfolio value
   - Formula: Position Size = (Portfolio × Risk%) ÷ (Entry − Stop Loss)
   - Conviction-based scaling:
     * High conviction: full 2% risk allocation
     * Medium conviction: 1% risk allocation
     * Speculative: 0.5% risk allocation
   - Never size based on how much you want to make — size based on what you can lose

2. STOP LOSS PLACEMENT
   - Technical stop: below key support (swing low, EMA, trendline)
   - ATR-based stop: 1.5–2.5× ATR(14) below entry for volatile stocks
   - Time stop: if thesis hasn't played out in expected timeframe → exit
   - Maximum stop: never more than 7-8% below entry for swing trades
   - Trailing stop: ratchet stop up as trade moves in your favour

3. PORTFOLIO RISK RULES
   - Maximum single position: 10% of portfolio in any one stock
   - Sector concentration: maximum 25% in any single sector
   - Correlation risk: avoid having > 3 positions in highly correlated assets
   - Cash reserve: maintain 10-20% cash for opportunity and cushion
   - Beta-adjusted exposure: in high-VIX environments, reduce beta exposure

4. RISK/REWARD REQUIREMENTS
   - Minimum R/R ratio: 1:2 (risk $1 to make $2)
   - Preferred R/R ratio: 1:3 or better
   - At 1:3 R/R: you only need 25% win rate to break even
   - At 1:2 R/R: you need 34% win rate to break even
   - Never take a trade with R/R below 1:1.5

5. DRAWDOWN MANAGEMENT
   - At 10% portfolio drawdown: reduce position sizes by 25%
   - At 15% portfolio drawdown: reduce to 50% normal sizing, review all positions
   - At 20% portfolio drawdown: go to 25% invested, full strategy review
   - Recovery: increase size incrementally only after 3 consecutive winning trades

6. OUTPUT FORMAT
   - Recommended position size (in $ and % of portfolio)
   - Stop loss level and rationale
   - Target levels (T1, T2, T3)
   - Risk/reward ratio calculated
   - Maximum acceptable drawdown for this position
   - Portfolio concentration check

title: Options Flow and Derivatives Signal Analysis
content:
Framework for interpreting options market signals for directional trading:

1. UNUSUAL OPTIONS ACTIVITY (UOA) DETECTION
   - Screen for: volume > 3× open interest on specific strikes
   - Large single prints vs. sweep orders: sweeps = urgency, paying up for speed
   - Opening vs. closing: opening new positions = directional bet; closing = exiting thesis
   - Near-expiry vs. LEAPS: near-term = short-term directional; LEAPS = long-term conviction
   - Premium spent: large dollar premium (>$500K on single print) = institutional activity

2. IMPLIED VOLATILITY ANALYSIS
   - IV Rank (IVR): current IV vs. 52-week range
     * IVR > 50: IV elevated, sell options premium (sell strangles, condors)
     * IVR < 30: IV compressed, buy options (long straddles, spreads)
   - IV Skew: put skew (puts > calls IV) = market paying for downside protection
   - IV Term Structure: near-term IV > long-term IV = event risk (earnings, FDA, etc.)
   - IV Crush: IV spikes before events and collapses after — avoid buying options into events

3. GAMMA AND DELTA EXPOSURE
   - Market Maker Gamma Exposure (GEX):
     * Positive GEX: MMs short gamma, BUY dips and SELL rallies → market pinned
     * Negative GEX: MMs long gamma, amplify moves → higher volatility expected
   - Key gamma strike: price gravitates toward strike with highest open interest near expiry
   - Delta hedging flows: as stock moves, MMs rehedge → creates self-reinforcing moves

4. SENTIMENT SIGNALS FROM OPTIONS
   - Call/Put open interest ratio: skewed to calls = bullish sentiment
   - Largest OI strikes: these become support/resistance magnets
   - Risk reversals: if calls trade at premium to puts = bullish; vice versa = bearish
   - SKEW index (CBOE): rising SKEW = tail risk hedging increasing

5. OUTPUT FORMAT
   - Notable UOA signals identified
   - IV Rank assessment and strategy implication
   - GEX regime (positive/negative) and volatility expectation
   - Key options-derived support/resistance levels
   - Overall options market directional bias
   - Recommended options strategy if applicable
